3 
PREFACE 
 
The advent of the Competence-Based Curriculum (CBC) heralds a new era of dynamic and 
practical learning experiences designed to equip learners with the 21st century skills. It marks 
a trans-formative step in improving education quality and relevance.  
 
The French Language Teaching Module has been designed in line with the Competence 
Based Education principles which seek to link education to real life experiences. The General 
Competences provided in the 2023 Zambia Curriculum Framework are linked to the content 
of this module.  
 
The module aims to support French teachers in the implementation of the Competence Based 
Curriculum in the absence of approved text books. It therefore reflects the Ministry’s 
unwavering commitment to making this journey as smooth as possible for teachers. However, 
teachers are encouraged to supplement this module with their own research and innovations 
in order to address any gaps that may arise during implementation. 
I hope teachers will make good use of this resource.  
 
Kelvin Mambwe (PhD.) 
Permanent Secretary- Educational Services 
MINISTRY OF EDUCATION

4 
ACKNOWLEDGEMENT 
The French Language Teaching Module has been developed through a consultative and 
participatory process. Many individuals, institutions and organisations were consulted and 
accorded an opportunity to make proposals about the content of this teaching module. I 
would, therefore, like to express my profound gratitude to individuals, institutions and 
organisations that provided the financial and technical input to the successful development of 
this teaching module. These include; Teachers, Lecturers from Colleges of Education and 
Universities, Subject Associations, Civil Society Organisations, and Cooperating Partners. 
Finally, I appreciate the commitment and hard work of the staff at the Directorate of 
Curriculum Development in ensuring the successful completion of the Teaching Module. 
 
Charles Ndakala, (Dr.) 
Director – Curriculum Development 
MINISTRY OF EDUCATION

6 
 
 
UNIT:  
6 
TOPIC 1.6.0:  ATTRIBUTES OF A PERSON OR THING 
 
Overview 
This topic discusses qualities, characteristics or features that define a person or thing. It 
explores physical attributes of a person such as height,weight, eye colour, complexion  and 
personality traits such as kindness, honesty, patience, confidence. The topic will also deal 
with physical characteristics for a thing namely, size, shape, colour and material. The topic 
has two sub-topics namely Colours and Physical Characteristics and Personality. 
General Competences  
Communication 
i. 
Use appropriate language  when describing a person or a thing in French. 
ii. Express oneself clearly, effectively and take turns when conversing with others. 
Emotional intelligence 
i. 
Learners express empathy for other people. 
ii. Learners will relate effectively with others as they discuss their different personalities. 
Hook 
Display a set of images of animals or objects depicting different emotions, sizes, colours and 
other qualities. Ask the learners to describe each one of them using adjectives they already 
know e.g  petit/e, grand, content. 
Sub -Topic 1.6.1 Colours 
Introduction

7 
This sub topic focuses on colours. Colours are one of the first vocabulary sets learners learn. 
They are easy to visualize and use in everyday life, making them a great starting point for 
language learning. Knowing colours helps learners to describe objects, clothes, food and the 
world around them. This enhances the ability to speak and understand French in real life 
situations. Learning about colours will also enable  learners to understand the agreement in 
gender and number, an essential part of French grammar. 
Specific Competence 1.6.1.1: Express preferences for colours in French. 
Key Terms /Words/Vocabulary  
Le vocabulaire 
Les couleurs - vert/verte, rouge, orange, noir/noire blanc, blanche (masculin/féminin)  rouge, 
jaune, bleu/bleue, brun/brune, marron,  rose  
Les expressions:  
Décrire la couleur - Il/elle est + nom de couleur  
(Il est noir, le chien) , Cʹest + nom de couleur  
Le grammaire: 
Les verbes - aimer, préférer, colorer, décolorer, peindre, être, avoir. 
Learning Activities: 
Activity 1. Identifying colours of objects (rouge, jaune, verte) 
  Provide a dialogue on colours. 
E.g. 
Monique: Quelle est ta couleur préférée?   
Jacques: Ma couleur préférée est le bleu, et toi? 
Monique : Moi, j’aime le blanc.  
Jacques:Tu aimes le rouge aussi? 
Monique: No. Je n’aime pas le rouge.

8 
 Learners listen to a dialogue/conversation on colours and identify the names of colours in 
French. 
 Provide different objects with colours mentioned in the dialogue for learners to see. 
 Introduce other colours to the learners (vert/e, gris, jaune, orange etc). 
 
Activity 2: Discussing one’s preferences for colours (j’aime/ je n’aime pas la couleur verte) 
Content tips:  
 Colours that end with ‘-e’ do not change form according to gender e.g le pantalon rouge, 
la robe rouge. 
 Some colours agree in gender and number with the noun e.g  
Un sac bleu - des sacs bleus 
Une robe verte - des robes vertes 
 If a noun is feminine, many colour adjectives take an ‘-e’ at the end e.g une robe bleue, 
une pomme verte 
 Some colours change form to agree with the noun’s gender and number e.g blanc(m)/ 
blanche(f) e.g un mur blanc, une robe blanche 
  Conjugate le verbe ‘aimer’ and préférer. 
 Explain  la négation ‘ne…. pas’ structure. 
 
In groups, learners talk about colour preferences using the verbs ‘aimer’ and ‘préférer 
(positive and negative form). 
 
Learners construct sentences using ‘ne … pas’ for their colour preferences. 
Activity 3. Drawing and coloring the Flag of Zambia. 
 
Learners mention colours of the Flag of Zambia in French. 
 
Learners draw the Flag of Zambia and label the colours in French. 
Expected Standard: Preferences for colours expressed in French correctly. 
Key Points/Recap

9 
i. 
Colours in French. 
ii. Gender and number for colours. 
Specific Competence1.6.1.2: Distiguish gender in colours. 
Learning Activities 
Activity 1: Describing the colour of an object or thing (vert/e, blanc /he). 
Content Tips 
 Some colours change form to agree with the noun’s gender and number e.g blanc(m)/ 
blanche(f) e.g un mur blanc, une robe blanche. 
 
Provide objects or items of different colours. 
 
Learners describe the colours of objects or items in French. 
Activity 2: Writing dictated words on colours (masculine/feminine). 
 
Learners write dictated words on colours. 
Expected Standard: Preferences for colours expressed in French correctly. 
Key Points / Recap  
i. 
Gender of colours. 
Sub-Topic 1.6.2  Physical Characteristics and Personality 
Introduction  
This subtopic  will enhance learners’ communication skills,  understanding of cultural 
contexts and will enable them express  themselves with confidence as they describe 
characteristics of a person and objects by using adjectives related  to traits  in French.

10 
Specific Competence 1.6.2.1 Describe someone’s physical characteristics and personality in 
French. 
 Key Terms /Words/Vocabulary  
Le vocabulaire 
la tête, les cheveux, les yeux, le nez, les oreilles, la bouche . 
Les expressions 
 Décrire quelqu’un : Il/Elle est comment ?/ Comment est-il /elle?  
 Il /elle est … (petit/petite,/grand/grande/, gros/ grosse, mince / Il(elle) est très … beau/belle, 
etc. il /elle est un peu, assez …. Il/elle a les cheveux longs et noirs. 
Grammaire 
 Le comparatif -  Il/Elle est plus +(adjectif) que, Il/Elle est moins+(adjectif) que … 
Les verbes -  être, avoir 
 Learning Activities 
Activity 1: Identifying physical characteristics and personality   (cheveux longs, les yeux 
bruns). 
 
Draw ‘le corps humain’ and label the external parts in French (la tête, les cheveux, les 
yeux, le nez, les oreilles, la bouche etc). 
 
Learners read and pronounce the body parts in French correctly.  
 
In groups, learners identify the physical characteristics of people from the images or 
pictures displayed. E.g Il est petit/elle est petite, Il est  grand/elle est grande. 
 
Activity 2: Discussing adjectives in French. 
Content Tips:  
 Add an ‘e’ to the describing word if the word being described is feminine e.g la grande 
fille, le grand garçon,/ add ‘s’ to the describing word if the word being described is plural 
e.g  les grands garçons, les grandes filles 
 In English, you can use the same describing word (adjective) for whatever  
you  
like 
to describe but in French the describing word has to change to  match  whether what it is

11 
describing is masculine/feminine/singular/plural (adjectives  
must agree with the thing 
they are describing) 
 
Provide sentences in French in which adjectives describing physical characteristics of a 
person or thing have been used. 
E. g. C’est une petite maison. Il est un beau garçon. 
 
Learners identify adjectives used in the given sentences. 
 
Activity 3: Describing a person or an object using information provided (cheveux longs, les 
 
yeux bruns, petit(e), grand(e)). 
 
In pairs, learners describe a friend or family members using the vocabulary learned. 
 
Write a guided composition on describing a person using a picture. 
Content Tips 
 Adjectives ending in ‘e’ don’t need to add an extra ‘e’ when dealing with gender e.g  
jeune/jeune, mince /mince. 
 
 Discuss irregular adjectives (adjectives ending in - x,-f, -er, -on, -en, -el, and  il.  
Group of 
words 
ending 
Example  
Mas.singl Fem. 
singl 
Mas. pl 
Fem. pl 
- x 
Heureux (happy) 
heureux 
heureuse 
heureux 
heureuses 
- f 
Neuf (new) 
neuf 
neuve 
neufs 
neuves 
- er 
Premier (first) 
premier 
première 
premiers 
premières 
- on,- en, 
-el - il  
Bon (good) 
bon 
bonne 
bons 
bonnes 
- c 
Blanc (white) 
blanc 
blanche 
blancs 
blanches 
-au/el 
Beau(handsome)Belle(beautiful) beau 
belle 
beaux 
belles 
Activity 4: Discussing the expressions used to describe ones personality.  
 
Introduce adjectives used to describe ones personality (heureux, intelligent, content/e, 
paresseux). 
 
Learners role-play describing someone’s personality e.g.  Mon frère est calme, Ma mère 
est timide.

12 
Activity 5: Formulating adverbs from  adjectives (timide…timidement, gentil…gentiment, 
 
prudent…prudemment 
Content Tips 
 Adverbs can be divided in four groups that describe : how something happens (les 
adverbes de manière), where it happens (les adverbes de lieu), when it happens (les 
adverbes de temps) and to what extent (les adverbes d’intensité) 
 In French most adverbs of manner are formed by adding - ment to the feminine form of 
adjective e.g douce - doucement 
 If an adjective  ends in  a vowel add - ment to the masculine form e.g joli - joliment 
 Adjectives ending in - ent or ant change to emment and  amment ( both pronounced  
amant) e.g prudent - prudemment/ brillant - brillamment. Exceptions: lent - lentement 
 
Learners formulate adverbs from given adjectives. 
Activity 6: 
Completing sentences with correct adjectives and adverbs. 
 
Learners complete the given passage with the correct adjectives and adverbs.  
 
 Expected Standard: Physical characteristics and personalities  described correctly .  
Assessment  
 
 Note that assessment will be done during and after each learning activity.  
 
Provide assessment for each learning activity which is meant to achieve the specific 
competence.   
 
The expected standard will be judged by the formative assessment for the learning 
activities from all the sub-topics taught and the summative assessment given at the end of 
the each topic  
 
The assessment activities shall include project based tasks, practical demonstration, 
written assessment (quizzes, open ended and closed questions…) 
Key Points /Recap 
i. 
Adjectives:  l’ accord mas./fem.(m/f agreement),  l’ accord singl/pl (sing./pl agreement). 
ii. Adverbs: la formation (forming adverbs).

13 
Teaching and Learning materials: Tempo 1/Alter ego

14 
 
UNIT:  
7 
TOPIC 1.7.0:  NUMERACY 
 
 
Overview 
 
Numeracy refers to the ability to understand, interpret and work with numbers and 
mathematical concepts. Numbers are a basic and essential part of any language. They appear 
in everyday conversation, from telling time and dates to handling money, giving phone 
numbers or counting items. Understanding numbers in French helps learners to engage in 
practical communication through practical usage of numbers in real life situations. Learning 
numbers in French also enables learners to know how French speakers structure numbers in a 
unique way. This topic has three sub-topics namely, Cardinal Numbers, Ordinal Numbers and 
Counting Money. 
 
General Competences 
 
Communication 
i. 
Learners will use numbers  during their everyday conversation in different situations. 
Critical Thinking 
(i) 
Learners  will explore how French speakers structure numbers in a unique way there 
 
by recognizing numbers and their pattern relationships. 
Problem Solving: 
(i) 
Learners will develop skills that help them budget, save and make smart financial 
decisions. 
 
Hook:   
Imagine you are in Paris, standing in a bakery and want to buy two croissants. The baker 
asks, ‘combien en voulez - vous?’ you know what you want, but how do you say the number 
in French? Without your numbers in French, you might end up with twelve instead of two.  
 
Sub - Topic 1.7.1: Cardinal Numbers 1-1000 in French 
Introduction

15 
Cardinal numbers are used constantly in everyday life  when expressing age, quantity, prices, 
time and dates. They are building blocks for learning ordinal numbers as they are a 
foundation for other number types and mathematical operations. 
Specific Competence 1.7.1.1:  Count from 0 - 1000 in French. 
 
Key Terms /Words-        
 
Le vocabulaire 
Chiffre, zéro, un, deux, cent, trois cent, mille, deux mille, numéro, combien, c’est…  
Le grammaire  
Les verbes: compter, demander. 
 
Learning Activities     
 
 
 
 
 
Activity  1:  Listening to numbers in French.  
 
 Teacher counts objects or items in French. E.g. un, deux, trois, quatre, cinq … 
  Learners listen and count after the teacher with correct pronunciation. 
  Display a chart with objects and their numbers in French. E.g. one pen: Un stylo, ten  
oranges: Dix oranges… 
 In groups, learners count different items in French.  
 
 
 
 
Activity  2: Identifying numbers from 0 to 1000 in French. 
 
Content Tips 
 Numbers from 11 (onze)  to 16 (seize) end with ‘ze’  
 Numbers from 17 to 19 are ten - seven (dix-sept), ten-eight (dix-huit) and ten-nine (dix-
neuf). 
 30, 40, 50 and 60 in french end with ‘nte’ (trente, quarante, cinquante, soixante) 
 70 is sixty-ten (soixante-dix) 
 80 is four 20s (quatre-vingts) 
 90 is four 20s-ten (quatre-vingts-dix)

16 
 In between numbers are like in English e.g. vingt-deux, vingt-trois. Except for (21: vingt 
et un, 31: trente et un, 41: quarante et un…) 
 Display a chart with numbers in French (zéro to cent) 
 Learners identify the numbers ending with -ze-, -nte-… and group them accordingly. 
 
Activity 3: Counting cardinal numbers from  0  to 1000. 
 Learners count from 0 to 100. 
 In groups, learners use flashcards to compete in identifying numbers in French. 
 In pairs, learners ask each other’s phone number in French.  
 
Pierre : Bonjour. 
 
Irène : Bonjour. Quel est ton numéro de téléphone. 
 
Pierre : C’ est 0974 60-82-89; et toi? 
 
Irène : Moi, c’est 0974 15-84-21. 
 
Activity  4:  Write numbers in French from- 0 – 1000. 
 
 Learners write numbers in French from- 0 - 100. 
 Learners match the numbers in figures to numbers in French. 
 
Expected Standard:  Cardinal numbers counted correctly. 
 
Key Points / Recap      
 
i. 
Counting cardinal numbers in French. 
ii. Writing numbers in French. 
 
Sub-Topic   1. 7. 2  Ordinal Numbers 
Introduction 
In French, ordinal numbers are very important as they help learners to know the position, 
ranking and order. Ordinal numbers help learners form more natural sentences and 
improve their ability to understand instructions, sequence and narratives.

17 
Specific  Competence  1 .7.2.1: Demonstrate knowledge of ordinal numbers from 1 to 20 in 
French. 
Key Terms /Words/ Vocabulary 
Le vocabulaire: 
Les nombres ordinaux: premier/première, deuxième … 
Learning Activities  
Activity 1: Listening to a passage on ordinal numbers and identify them. E.g. (premier , 
deuxième  etc…). 
 Provide a passage or dialogue which contains ordinal numbers. E.g. premier, 
deuxième, … 
 Learners  identify  ordinal  numbers from the passage or dialogue. 
 Explain the meaning of ordinal numbers used in the passage or dialogue. 
 
Activity2: Discussing Ordinal numbers and their endings- (ième). 
Content tips: 
 Explain the formation of Ordinal numbers. 
 Add -ième to the cardinal number to get deuxième (second), troisième (third), etc. 
 Explain the complete change for un/ une into masculin : premier/feminin : première 
(first) and spelling adjustment for cinq - cinquième (fith), neuf - neuvième (ninth). 
Words ending in ‘e’such as quatre, sieze, treize... drop the ‘e’to become quatrième, 
seizième, and treizième respectively.  
 Learners read and pronounce the Ordinal numbers. 
 
Activity 3: Reading the passage on ordinal numbers and answer the questions. 
 Provide passage /dialogue for learners to read and pronounce the ordinal numbers.

18 
 In groups, learners write ordinal numbers from different passages /dialogues 
provided. 
 Groups present their findings. 
 
Activity 4: Writing ordinal numbers from 1 to 20.  
 Learners  write  ordinal numbers from 1 to  20 
 
Expected Standard :  Knowledge of ordinal numbers 1 to 20 demonstrated correctly. 
Key Points / Recap 
i. 
Formation of ordinal numbers. 
ii. Reading and pronouncing ordinal numbers. 
iii. Writing ordinal numbers in French. 
 
Sub-Topic  1. 7. 3      Counting Money 
Introduction 
Money management is an important life skill. By learning about counting money, learners 
will develop skills that help them budget, save and make smart financial decisions. This sub-
topic will also help learners explore mathematical operations and develop skills to manage 
everyday transactions with confidence. 
 Specific Competence  1.7.3.1  Apply the four mathematical operations on numbers below 
100 in French. 
Key Terms /words  
Le vocabulaire 
Combien coûte, l’argent, le prix, la monnaie, égale. 
Le grammaire 
Les verbes - compter, demander, acheter, diviser (divisé), multiplier (fois), ajouter (plus), 
soustraire (moins). 
Learning Activities  
Activity1: Adding, Subtracting, Dividing  and Multiplying small numbers.

19 
Content Tips 
  Explain the four mathematical operations - divisé, fois, plus, moins. 
a) Adding small numbers and amounts   
e.g 2 + 3= 5 (Deux plus trois égale cinq). 
K10 + K5 = K15 (Dix Kwacha plus Cinq Kwacha égale Quinze Kwacha). 
 Provide a scenario on  addition 
e.g. J’ achête un livre à K20 et deux stylos à K15. Le transport est K 5. Combien 
 
 
  est le total?  
 
b) Subtracting small numbers and amounts 
e.g 7 - 2 = 5 (sept moins deux égale cinq). 
K34 - K11 = K23 (Trente quatre kwacha moins onze kwacha égale vingt trois kwacha). 
 Provide a scenario on  substraction.  
e.g. J’ai K50. j’ achête un crayon à K5, deux stylos à K10 et trois livres à K30. Je 
 reste avec combien d’ argent.  
c) Multiplying small numbers and amounts 
e..g 8 x 3 = 24 (huit fois trois égale vingt-quatre) 
K6 x 5 = K30 (six kwacha fois cinq égale trente kwacha) 
 Provide a scenario on  multiplication 
e.g. Je veux acheter huit oranges. Une orange coûte K5. Combien coûtent huit 
oranges? 
 
d) Dividing small numbers and amounts. 
e.g 12 ÷ 4 = 3 (Douze divisé par quatre égale trois). 
K 32 ÷ 16 = K2 (Trente deux Kwacha divisé par seize égale deux Kwacha). 
 Provide a scenario on division. 
e.g. J’ai K 27. Un bonbon coûte K3. Combien de bombons je vais acheter dans K27?

20 
  
Activity 2: Writing a small budget in French. 
 Provide a  sample of a written budget.  
e.g. Fait un budget mensuel. 
 
 
 
 
 
Catégorie 
dépense 
Nourriture 
K 100 
Transports 
K 40 
vêtements 
K50 
Téléphone / internet 
K 20 
Lotion 
K15 
Total 
K 225 
 
 Learners write a small budget in French.  
e.g. Quotidien ou mensuel. 
 
Expected Standard: The four mathematical operations applied correctly. 
Assessment 
 
Note that assessment will be done during and after each learning activity.  
 
 Provide assessment for each learning activity which is meant to achieve the specific  
competence. 
 
The expected standard will be judged by the formative assessment for the learning  
activities from all the sub-topics taught and the summative assessment given at the end  
of the each topic  
 · The assessment activities shall include project based tasks, practical demonstration, 
written assessment (quizzes, open ended and closed questions…) 
Key points/ Recap:  
i. 
Use of Addition ,Subtraction, Division and Multiplication in French 
ii. Writing a simple budget in French 
Teaching and Learning Materials: Tempo 1, GSCE French, Luangwa 2

21 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
UNIT:  
8 
TOPIC 1.8.0:  PREFERENCES

22 
 
Overview 
Expressing one’s preferences is essential for effective daily communication. This skill 
enables learners to share their interests and engage with others, which is crucial for building 
relationships. This topic will cover essential vocabulary, key phrases, and sentence structures 
necessary for discussing preferences. Additionally, it will introduce cultural aspects of 
preferences in the French-speaking world, such as common food, leisure activities, and social 
norms surrounding likes and dislikes. By the end, learners will be able to articulate their likes 
and dislikes in various contexts, thereby increasing their confidence in speaking French. This 
topic has one sub topic. 
General Competences 
Communication 
i. 
Develop basic conversational skills to express likes, dislikes, and preferences clearly and 
confidently in French. 
Emotional Intelligence 
(i) 
Understand how preferences reflect personality and emotions.  
Show empathy towards others when discussing likes and dislikes. 
Critical Thinking 
i. 
Analyse the reasons behind personal preferences and formulate responses that express 
these reasons in French, e.g., "Je préfère les films comiques parce qu'ils me font rire" 
ii. Recognize the role of language in shaping and conveying personal preferences, and how 
these may vary across cultures. 
iii. Compare and contrast preferences with classmates in order to foster a deeper 
understanding and appreciation of cultural diversity within French-speaking 
communities. 
Hook: 
Ask learners questions about things they like or dislike. (e.g Qui préfère le chocolat/les 
bonbons?

23 
 
Sub-Topic 1.8.1 Likes and Dislikes 
Introduction  
The subtopic focuses on how to express likes and dislikes in French. Being able to clearly 
communicate what you like or dislike is essential for both social interactions and personal 
conversations. By learning these expressions, learners will be able to express their opinions 
and preferences with confidence. 
Specific Competence 1.8.1.1: Express likes and dislikes in French.  
 Key Terms/Words/Vocabulary 
Le vocabulaire 
corruption, mauvais comportement, fromage, glace, campagne, chocolat 
Les expressions  
Qu’est-ce que tu aimes ? Quel est ton plat préféré ? Mon passe-temps préféré est…faire de la  
randonnée,  faire du vélo, jouer au foot, faire un pique – nique, faire de la photographie… 
 
Le grammaire 
La négation; ne ....pas (ex...: je nʹaime pas) /oui/ non/Si/ On = nous/ Je n’ aime pas 
beaucoup... /Je n’aime pas du tout /Je déteste... les pronoms démonstratifs; celui-ci/celui-là 
/celle-ci/celle-là / ceux-ci/ceux-là 
Les verbes - aimer, admirer, détester, déplaire, dégoûter, rejeter, préférer, adorer etc. 
Les adverbes: beaucoup, un peu etc 
 
 
 
Learning Activities

24 
Activity 1: Responding to oral questions on likes and dislikes in French (Qu’est ce que tu   
aimes? J’aime/ je n’aime pas le chocolat...). 
 
Provide verbs and phrases used to express likes and dislikes (e.g., J’aime, Je déteste, 
Je préfère, Je n’aime pas…).  
 
Conjugate the verbs : Aimer, détester, adorer… au temps présent. 
 
Introduce question words: Qu’est – ce que, quel/quelle est/qui... ? 
 
In pairs, learners practice asking and answering simple questions about their 
preferences using the verbs and phrases provided.  
a) Likes  
e.g.  
Henri: Qu’est-ce que tu aimes?  
Marie: J’aime le chocolat.  
Henri: Quel est ton plat préféré ? 
Marie: Je préfère Nsima au poulet/… 
Henri: Qui aime les bonbons ? 
Henri: Moi. 
b) Dislikes 
e.g.  
Susanne: Qu’est ce que tu détestes ? 
 
André: Je déteste les bananes. 
Content Tips 
 Explain the negative expression: ‘ne … pas’ can also be used to express dislikes (subject 
+ ne + verbe + pas). 
e.g.  
Michel: Tu aimes les carottes ? Pauline: Non, je n’aime pas les carottes. 
Activity 2: Expressing likes and dislikes for taste, colour, hobbies, places, buildings, scenery, 
people. 
 Provide a passage/ dialogue on likes and dislikes about different things and objects 
e.g. colours, hobbies, places, food … .

25 
 Learners read and identify different preference expressions/ phrases. 
 Provide more examples to help learners express likes and dislikes for each category 
listed. 
e.g:  
i. 
Taste: j’adore la glace, je déteste le fromage, etc. 
ii. 
Colour: j’aime le bleu, je n’aime pas le rouge, etc. 
iii. 
Hobbies: j’aime lire, je n’aime pas danser, j’adore jouer au foot, etc. 
iv. 
Places: j’aime la plage, j’adore les montagnes, je déteste les grandes villes, 
etc. 
v. 
Buildings: j’aime les musées, je n’aime pas les hôtels etc. 
vi. 
Scenery: j’aime la mer, je déteste les déserts, j’adore la campagne, etc. 
vii. 
People: j’adore ma famille, je déteste les personnes impolies, etc. 
 In pairs, learners practice expressions used to ask about likes and dislikes. 
e.g.  
Quel est ton plat préféré? / Qu’est-ce que tu aimes manger ? 
 
Quelle est ta couleur préférée ? 
 
Quelle est ton passe-temps préféré/ favori ? 
 
Est-ce que tu aimes les musées/la campagne/ le cinéma ? 
 
Qu’est-ce que tu aimes dans la vie ?  
 
Quel sport tu préfères/ quel sport préfères- tu?  
 In small groups, learners discuss likes and dislikes for taste, colour, hobbies, places, 
buildings, scenery, people. 
 
Activity 3: Reading a passage on likes and dislikes and answering questions based on the 
passage. 
 Provide learners with a passage on likes and dislikes. 
 Learners read the passage and answer questions. 
 
Activity 4: Writing about likes and dislikes in French. 
 Learners write a coherent paragraph on their likes and dislikes. 
 
Expected Standard: Likes and dislikes expressed accurately.

26 
Assessment 
 Note that assessment will be done during and after each learning activity. 
  Provide assessment for each learning activity which is meant to achieve the 
specific competence. 
 The expected standard will be judged by the formative assessment for the 
learning activities from all the sub-topics taught and the summative assessment 
given at the end of each topic  
 The assessment activities shall include project based tasks, practical 
demonstration, written assessment (quizzes, open ended and closed 
questions…)  
Key Points Recap 
 
Key Vocabulary for Likes and Dislikes. 
 
Negation: Expressing dislikes. 
 
Sentence Structure: Subject + Verb + Object/Activity/Preference. 
Teaching and Learning Materials: Tempo 1/ Latitude 1/Contact 1. 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
UNIT:  
9 
TOPIC 1.9.0:  LOCALISATION AND MOVEMENT

27 
 
Overview:  
In French, localisation refers to the concept of ‘where’ something is, while movement 
involves the concept of ‘where something is going’ or ‘how it is moving’. This topic 
discusses the position of an object, person, or thing in a sentence. It is a crucial topic for 
learners because it equips them with the vocabulary, expressions and grammatical structures 
needed for describing position, direction and distance.  
General Competences 
Communication 
i. 
Apply listening ,speaking ,reading ,and writing skills. 
ii. Express oneself (spoken and written) clearly and effectively using prepositions of place. 
iii. Express oneself  using different media and symbols of something object or person. 
 
Analytical thinking 
(i) 
Use appropriate language (Verbal and non – verbal in different situations when giving 
directions and locating the position of something. 
Emotional Intelligence:  
(ii) 
Learners will use appropriate expressions and show empathy when asking for 
direction. 
Hook: 
How do you ask for direction and location of a place you have never been to? 
 
Sub -Topic 1.9.1. Position  
Introduction: 
Position is defined as the location or placement of an object, person, and point in relation to 
its surrounding. It describes where something is situated physically. 
Specific Competence 1.9.1.1: Describe the position of an object in French. 
Key-Terms/ Words:  
Le vocabulaire:

28 
Ici, là, devant, entre, sur, sous, dans, derrière, à côté de, au-dessus, en dessous, près, loin, 
en face de, à gauche, à droite ,en arrière,  en avant, au  Nord,  Sud, Est, Ouest, à, o, en… 
Le grammaire: 
Les verbes - aller, venir, tourner, prendre, faire, revenir… 
 
Learning Activities 
Activity 1: Identifying preposition of place from a given passage/ demonstration (sur, dans 
…). 
 Provide a passage with prepositions of place. 
 Learners read the passage on prepositions of place (sur, dans, sous …). 
 Demonstrate and explain the meaning of each preposition used in the passage by 
using objects in class with different prepositions of place. 
e.g Le stylo est sur la table. 
Le sac est sous la chaise. 
Les élèves sont en classe. 
Le chien est dans la cour. 
 
 Learners identify different positions of objects in a given picture. 
 
Activity 2: Discussing the position of the object in relation to another. 
 Introduce other prepositions and explain their meaning to the learners. e.g. derrière, 
devant, à côté de … . 
 Provide any two objects to be described in relation to each other. Position the two 
objects. e.g la chaise /table. 
 Ask the position of the chair. (Où est la chaise?) e. g  la chaise est à côté de la table.  
 Put different objects in different places and ask learners to describe their positions. 
   
Activity 2: Discussing the position of the object in relation to another. 
 Identify the two objects to be described in relation to each other.  
 Learners name the object e. g le stylo , la table.

29 
 Position the two objects (le stylo est sur la table ) 
 Ask learners the position of le stylo .( où est le stylo?) e. g  le stylo est sur la table.  
 Introduce other prepositions of place  and explain their meaning to the learners .e.g 
sous, devant … . 
 Place different objects in different positions in relation to the other by using (devant, a 
cote, derriere, au, dessous). 
 Learners construct sentences on the position of an object in relation to the other. 
 
Expected Standard: Position of an object described correctly. 
 
Key Points/Recap 
i. 
Prepositions of place. 
 
Specific competence 1.9.1.2:  State the location of a person, place or thing in French. 
Learning Activities 
Activity 1: Locating a place or person on a given map or drawing (notre  école se trouve / est 
près de la banque. Ma mère est devant toi…) 
 Provide a map or a chart with people, buildings, roads and symbols correctly marked. 
 Learners identify landmarks such as hôtel, restaurant, banque, hopital, Rue Cairo… 
on the map by naming them. 
 Learners identify where a person, place or thing is located on the map in relation to 
buildings, roads … 
 e.g.  
Où est Marie ?  
Marie est devant le café. 
Où est l’école Milemu? 
L’école Milemu est près du restaurant. 
 Explain how to use ‘se trouver’ in a sentence when giving a response.                      
e.g. Notre école se trouve près de la banque.

30 
 Learners respond to questions on positions of people, places or things using the map 
and the vocabulary learned (près, devant derrière, en face de, à côté de…). 
 
Activity 2: Discussing the campus (l’Est, Ouest, Nord, Sud). 
 Introduce the four cardinal points of the campus (le Sud, le Nord, l’Est, l’Ouest). 
 Learners draw the campus and label the points in French. 
 
Activity 3:Locating the provinces of Zambia and their headquarters (Province  du nord-
Kasama, province du sud-choma, province du nord-ouest, solwezi). 
 Learners name the 10 provinces of Zambia and their headquarters in French. 
 Learners match provinces to different activities found in each province  
e.g province de l’Est - Nc’wala. 
Expected Standard: Location of a thing, place, and people stated in French accordingly. 
Key Points /Recap 
i. 
Prepositions used to express position. 
ii. The campus. 
 
Sub-topic 1.9.2 Direction 
Introduction 
Directions are instructions or guidance on how to navigate from one place to another. They 
help learners understand where they are, where they need to go, and how to get there. Asking 
for and giving directions in French is an essential skill used in everyday conversations. This 
sub-topic discusses how to ask, give direction and locate a place through learning the basic 
phrases and vocabulary used in the given context. 
Specific competence: 1.9.2.1 Give directions to someone in French. 
Key Terms/Words/ Vocabulary 
 Le vocabulaire:

31 
Est, Ouest, Nord, Sud, allez tout droit, tournez à gauche. 
Le grammaire: 
Impératif /Vous de politesse, …. L’emploi de :à…, à l’…, à la… au … 
Les verbes - aller, tourner, monter, habiter, traverser 
 
Learning Activities 
Activity 1- Listening to a dialogue on giving directions and identifying expressions used 
(allez tout droit, tournez à gauche …). 
 Provide dialogue/conversation in which two people are asking for and giving 
directions.  
 Learners listen to the conversation or dialogue and identify expressions used when 
asking and giving directions e.g. Où se trouve l’hôpital? allez tout droit, tournez à 
gauche… 
 Demonstrate giving directions by using a sketch map with places, roads and symbols 
(aller tout droit, tourner à gauche …). 
 Learners practice giving direction using specific destinations of their choice. 
 
Activity 2: Reading a simple map and locating a place/ village/town/ any other /features. 
 Introduce more expressions used to ask for and give directions in French.  
Pour demander la direction:  
Où se trouve l’hôpital/ le parc ...?  
Où est l’église s’il vous plaît? 
Excusez-moi, Je cherche la banque ZANACO. 
Je suis perdu(e), c’est où le centre commercial? 
Reponse:  
Allez tout droit 
Continuez tout droit  
Tournez à droite (à gauche) 
Prenez la première route à droite

32 
Prenez à gauche (à droite)  
Traversez la rue (le pont) 
 
Content Tips: 
 Explain l’emploi de : à…, à l’…, à la… au … 
 
 Provide and display a map showing different places and ask learners to locate the 
places mentioned. 
 Learners role play asking for and giving directions using the expressions learned. 
  
Activity 3: Directing someone to a given place. 
 In pairs, learners ask for and give directions using correct expressions learned. 
 
Activity 4: Giving directions to ones own place of choice in written. 
 Learners write a guided composition directing someone to their own place of choice. 
 
Expected standard: Directions given to someone in French precisely. 
Key Points /Recap 
i. 
Expressions used to ask for and give directions 
ii. L’impératif /Vous de politesse, …. L’emploi de : à…, à l’…, à la… au … 
 
Sub-Topic 1.9.3 Distance 
Introduction 
Distance is a common topic in everyday conversations. Whether someone is asking how far a 
location is or discussing travel plans, knowing how to express distance allows one to engage 
in these conversations naturally. This sub-topic will discuss French expressions that are 
important in describing locations and directions. 
Specific competence: 1.9.3.1 Indicate how far or near a point is from another in French

33 
Key Terms/Words/ Vocabulary 
Le vocabulaire:  
Route, village, ville, direction, coin, cinéma, poste, banque, hôtel, église etc..petit/petite, 
grand/grande… le kilomètre, la distance, la rue, le mètre,  la hauteur, le lieu 
Les expressions: 
La distance - Prés de, loin de, dix kilomètres de … A quelle distance de…se trouve… ?/ 
C’est à quelle distance ? A combien de kilomètres de+nom de lieu? … 47 /où?se trouve…?  il 
y a ...kilomètres de …à…/c’est à... kilomètres de...à… C’est à…km de…  
Le grammaire:  
L’emploi de :à…, à l’…, à la… au … 
Les verbes: aller, tourner, monter, habiter, traverser 
Learning Activities 
Activity 1: Identifying expressions indicating how far or near a point is from another (prés 
de, loin de, dix kilometre de …) 
 Provide expressions used to talk about distance in French. 
Comment demander: 
À quelle distance de Lusaka se trouve Solwezi ? 
C’est à quelle distance ?  
À combien de kilomètres de Chinsali? 
Reponse: 
 Il y a 7 kilomètres de Chipata à Katete. 
C’est à 20 kilomètres de Choma à Mazabuka. 
 Learners  identify expressions used to indicate distance between two points  from a 
given passage (prés de, loin de, dix kilometre de …). 
 
Activity 2: Discussing distance between two points (prés de, loin de, dix kilometre de …)  
 Display a plan or map showing distances between two places.

34 
 Learners discuss how far the two places are from each other (prés de, loin de, à dix 
kilomètres de …). 
 In groups, learners construct sentences using “prés de, loin de, à … kilomètre de…”. 
 In pairs, learners role-play using expressions used to indicate distance between two 
points in French. 
Expected standard: Distance between two points indicated clearly. 
Assessment 
 Note that assessment will be done during and after each learning activity. 
  Provide assessment for each learning activity which is meant to achieve the 
specific competence. 
 The expected standard will be judged by the formative assessment for the 
learning activities from all the sub-topics taught and the summative assessment 
given at the end of each topic  
 The assessment activities shall include project based tasks, practical 
demonstration, written assessment (quizzes, open ended and closed 
questions…)  
Key Points/ Recap  
i. 
Expressions used to indicate distance. 
Teaching and Learning Materials: Tempo 1, Equipe 1 
 
 
 
 
UNIT: 10 
TOPIC 1.10.0: ACCOMMODATION AND HYGIENE 
 
Overview

35 
Accommodation and hygiene are two sides of the same coin that are closely linked to 
environment awareness. Hygiene plays a crucial role in ensuring good health, comfort and 
overall well-being. Understanding the importance of accommodation and hygiene helps 
learners to take care of their surroundings and make informed choices about where they live 
and how to maintain their environment. This topic will enrich learners’ vocabulary in French 
that will be used in their day to day lives. The topic has two sub-topics namely ‘Types of 
homes and Care for the surrounding and hygiene. 
General competences. 
Communication  
(i) 
Learners will acquire necessary vocabulary to engage in environmental discussions 
Critical thinking 
(i) 
Learners will develop environmental awareness that will enable them to make right 
decision on issues to do with their environment 
Environmental Sustainability:  
Learners will uphold good practices that benefit and sustain their environment  
 
Hook.  
Show two pictures showing dirty environment and ask learners to guess words for dirty and 
clean in French. 
Sub-Topic 1.10.1 Types of Homes. 
Introduction 
A home is a place where a person or family lives. It provides shelter, safety, comfort and a 
sense of belonging. A home can be a house, a hut or any space where people feel secure and 
at ease. It is more than just a building, it is where people eat, sleep, rest and spend time with 
loved ones. This sub topic explores different types of homes notably modern and traditional 
homes. 
Specific Competence1.10.1.1 Compare modern and traditional houses in French.

36 
 Key Terms/ Words/ Vocabulary.  
Le vocabulaire:  
Grande ou petite /à étages /blanche/ verte/ bleue/ jaune/ rouge/ en bois/en brique/en ciment/en 
terre battu …  
Le grammaire 
Les verbes: balayer, aérer, dormir, se coucher, sʹasseoir, nettoyer, prendre une douche, se 
baigner, laver, se laver, prévenir, jeter, boire, manger …  
Learning Activities. 
Activity 1: Identify the difference between a modern and traditional houses+ (en bois, en 
brique, en bous …).  
 Show pictures of different types of houses (traditional and modern). 
 Learners describe and identify modern and traditional houses in the pictures provided. 
(en bois, en brique, en bous ciment/en terre battue, en étage …). 
 Explain the traditional and modern types of houses in French. 
Traditionelle 
Une maison en bous. 
Une maison en bois. 
Une maison en terre battue. 
Moderne 
Une maison en brique. 
Une maison en étage. 
Content Tips  
 Explain: 
(i)  the structures 
Il y a (there is). Y a t-il? (is there?) 
(ii) the Prepositions  en ( made of ), au (à+ le) (at), Dans (in). 
Quels types des maisons y a t-il au village?

37 
Au village, il y a des maisons en bous/ bois. 
Dans mon village il y a des maisons en bous. 
 
 In groups, learners name the types of houses they know. 
 Learners draw and colour the two different types of houses. 
 Conclude with a matching activity. 
 Provide a list of material used for traditional and modern houses in French. 
 Learners classify the materials according to the types of houses. 
 
Activity 2: Reading a passage on modern and traditional houses and summarize the features 
of each type in French. 
 Provide a passage on characteristics of modern and traditional homes. 
 Learners read a given passage and answer the questions. 
 
Activity 3: Describing modern and traditional houses in writing. 
 Learners write a short-guided composition describing their homes or houses. 
 
Expected standard:  Modern and traditional houses compared in French correctly 
Key Points/ Recap ·  
i. 
Different types of houses. 
ii. Different materials used for building houses in French. 
iii. Structures: Il y a (there is). Y a t-il? (is there). 
iv. Prepositions: en ( made of ), au (à+ le) (at), Dans (in). 
 
Specific Competence: 1.10.1.2 Name different rooms and their use in a house. 
Key Terms/words/vocabulary 
Le vocabulaire:

38 
Les chambres, la chambre à coucher, le salon, la salle de bain, la salle à manger, la cuisine, 
les toilettes, la douche 
La grammaire 
Les verbes - Balayer, aérer, dormir, se coucher, sʹasseoir, nettoyer, prendre une douche, se 
baigner, laver, se laver, prévenir, jeter, boire, manger … . 
Learning Activities 
Acticity 1: Naming different rooms in a house and their use (chambre, salle à coucher) Les 
chambres.  
 Provide a sketch map of a house with different rooms  (salle à coucher, salon). 
 Explain the use of each room in the house (chambre - dormir, salon - regarder la 
télévision). 
 In groups, leaners name items found in each room e.g. Dans le salon il y’a une 
télévision, une table, une fauteuil. 
 Learners match different rooms with their corresponding uses. 
e.g,  
1. La chambre --------- dormir, étudier 
2. La salle de bain ------- se doucher 
 
Activity 2: Describing one’s own house in French (coleurs, taille, chambres … ) (written and 
orally) 
 Learners describe their houses and rooms by using different descriptive adjectives  
e .g ,'Comment est la maison ?’  Notre maison est … Ma chambre est … 
 
Activity 3: Writing a short-guided composition on a house of your choice. 
 Learners write a short composition on a house of their choice and describe the rooms. 
 
Expected Standard: Different rooms in a house and their use identified accordingly. 
Key Points/ Recap

39 
i. 
Names of different rooms in a house in French. 
ii. Items found in rooms. 
 
Sub- Topic 1.10.2 Care for the Surrounding. 
Introduction 
 
Living in a clean and conducive environment is key to good health. It is very important to 
take extra care of our surroundings in order to promote sound health. This sub topic focuses 
on the activities, hygiene behavioral changes and tools that are needed to maintain a good 
surrounding. 
 
Specific Competence 1.10.2.1 Describe a clean and dirty place. 
 
Key Terms/Words/ Vocabulary 
  
Le vocabulaire: 
La houe, la pelle, le balai, le torchon, l’eau, les déchets, le ménage, propre, sale, frais/fraîche, 
organise, le choléra, la toux, la poussière, poussiéreux, brillant, tache, odeur, la poubelle... 
La grammaire: 
Les verbes - nettoyer, ranger, faire, balayer, laver, arroser, fleurir, jardiner … 
 
Learning Activities 
Activity 1: Describing a clean and dirty place in French. 
 Provide two pictures. One showing a clean place and the other a dirty 
one. 
 Learners compare the two pictures provided in groups. 
 Explain the key words describing a clean and dirty place (propre, 
sale).

40 
 Learners describe the two pictures using the French vocabulary 
learned.  C’est propre, c’ est sale …  
Activity 2: Use the verb ‘être’ to indicate that a place is clean or dirty e.g la salle est propre,  
la salle est sale ou la chambre n’est pas propre. 
 In groups, learners discuss whether their class or school is clean or 
dirty in French 
e.g. clean: 
Notre classe est propre,  
Elle est aérée. 
Elle est fraiche 
Elle est organisée. 
 
e.g. dirty: 
Notre classe est sale 
Elle n’est pas aérée 
Elle n’est pas fraiche,  
Elle n’est pas organisée 
 Learners write a short paragraph describing a clean or a dirty place of 
their choice. 
Activity 2: Discussing ways of keeping the surrounding clean (balayer, nettoyer,  essuyer…). 
Content Tips: 
 Conjugate the verbs: balayer, travailler, nettoyer, faire and jeter in present tense. 
Balayer 
Travailler 
nettoyer 
jeter 
faire 
Je balaye 
Travaille 
Nettoie 
Jette 
Fais 
Tu balayes 
Travailles 
Nettoies 
Jettes 
Fais 
Il/elle balaye 
travaille 
nettoie 
jette 
Fait 
 
 Explain ways of keeping the surrounding clean e.g prendre soin de 
l’environnement, jeter les déchets dans la poubelle, éviter de jeter les 
déchets dans la rue, nettoyer la place, recycler les plastics, arroser les 
fleurs… . 
 Provide a passage on ways of  keeping a surrounding clean.

41 
 In groups, learners discuss the passage on ways of keeping the 
surrounding clean. (balayer chaque jour, nettoyer la cour, garder la 
propriété…). 
 Learners write a summary on  ways of keeping the surrounding clean. 
 
Activity 4: Identifying materials used for keeping the surrounding clean (balai, 
torchon, pelle …). 
 Provide a list of assorted items found in a house in French e.g 
télévision, radio, torchon, balai, houe, pelle, lit, … . 
 In groups, learners, identify the materials used to clean the 
surroundings from the provided list. 
 Introduce materials used for cleaning an environment. (le balai, le 
torchon, la houe, la pelle…). 
 Learners draw and label the cleaning materials in French. 
 Learners translate the learned vocabulary into English e.g torchon - 
mop.  
 
Activity 5: Stating negative effects of dirty surroundings. 
 Explain negative effects of dirty surroundings e.g. contracter les 
maladies comme le choléra, la toux, la dysenterie … . 
 Provide a text depicting the negative effects of dirty environments ( 
contracter le cholera, la toux, la diarrhée …). 
 In groups, learners read and discuss the passage on the negative effects 
of dirty surroundings. 
 Learners write a note summary on the negative effects of living in a 
dirty surroundings. 
 
  
Expected Standards: A clean and dirty place described correctly. 
Key Points/ Recap 
i. 
Expressions used to talk about a dirty and a clean place. 
ii. French vocabulary for cleaning materials. 
iii. Negative effects of dirty environments in French.

42 
 
Specific Competence: 1.10.2.2 Discuss personal hygiene. 
Key Terms/Words/ Vocabulary  
 
Le vocabulaire: 
Santé, sale, douche, se baigner, propre, hygiène, se laver, brosser les dents, parfum, 
brosse á dents, pâte dentifrice, savon, déodorant, ongles, cheveux, lait de beauté 
La grammaire: 
Les verbes - brosser, se/laver, se baigner, se doucher, couper… 
 
Learning Activities 
 
Activity 1: Mentioning ways of keeping oneself clean. (se laver, couper des cheveux, …). 
 Explain ways of keeping personal hygiene. (se laver, couper les 
cheveux, laver les mains…). 
 Guide learners on how to construct simple sentences on personal 
hygiene in French e.g laver les mains après la toilette, avant/après 
manger, se laver/ prendre la douche chaque jour, couper les ongles, 
coiffer les cheveux, brosser les dents chaque matin/ après manger … . 
 Provide a short passage on personal hygiene.  
 Learners answer questions based on the given passage. 
 Learners write a short paragraph on ways to keep oneself clean e.g. 
chaque matin, je me brosse les dents. Je me lave les mains après la 
toilette…) 
 
 
 
Activity 2: Drawing materials used for personal hygiene (le savon, la brosse à dent, 
de l’eau ...).

43 
 Provide a list of materials used for personal hygiene in French. (le 
savon, le dentifrice, l’eau …). 
 Learners draw and label materials used for personal hygiene and make 
talking walls or a photo gallery. 
 
Activity 3: Writing dictated words in French. 
 Learners write dictated words of the learned vocabulary on personal 
hygiene. 
Expected Standards: Personal hygiene discussed accordingly. 
Assessment  
 Note that assessment will be done during and after each learning activity.   
 Provide assessment for each learning activity which is meant to achieve the specific 
competence.  
 The expected standard will be judged by the formative assessment for the learning 
activities from all the sub-topics taught and the summative assessment given at the 
end of the each topic  
 The assessment activities shall include project based tasks, practical 
demonstration, written assessment (quizzes, open ended and closed questions…) 
 
Key Points/ Recap 
i. 
Ways of keeping personal hygiene. 
ii. Materials used for personal hygiene.  
 
Teaching and learning Materials: Tempo 1, Equipe 1
